memories.memory_text: I heard Dad’s voice outside the dorm supervisor’s room, asking to take me, like he had to come get me in the middle of the night because everything was falling apart.
grounded_facts: Matt King arrived at the dorm supervisor's room to retrieve Alexandra.
The event occurred at night, as indicated by the scene setting and the dorm supervisor appearing in a nightgown.
The dorm supervisor initially questioned whether Matt had contacted Administration before agreeing to help.

memories.memory_text: I was laughing because Dad showing up in his coat on the soccer field was so ridiculous — I couldn’t believe he actually came all the way out here.
grounded_facts: Alexandra sees her father arriving on the soccer field at night with the dorm supervisor.
She laughs and says, 'My fucking dad is here!' in a mocking, amused tone.
Her friend Emily is with her, also present during the encounter.

memories.memory_text: When he said I had to come home to see Mom, I threw my golf club into the dark — I don’t even know where it went — and just yelled, 'Fuck Mom!'
grounded_facts: Matt tells Alexandra she needs to come home to see her mother.
Alexandra reacts by throwing her golf club into the night.
She shouts 'Fuck Mom!' immediately after hearing the news.

memories.memory_text: I asked Matt how long I had to stay, because I could already feel the walls closing in and I didn’t owe him a performance just for showing up.
grounded_facts: Alexandra asks Matt
How long do I have to stay?
She speaks with clear impatience and detachment
The question follows a tense exchange about her behavior and lack of recognition for her efforts

memories.memory_text: I told him I was going swimming—not waiting for permission, not looking back—because the only thing worse than this family breakfast was sitting through more fake concern.
grounded_facts: Alexandra declares
I'm going swimming,
and gets up
Matt responds by offering to join her
She does not acknowledge his offer
showing disinterest in his presence
The statement follows her frustration about being unacknowledged

memories.memory_text: I told Dad, 'You really don't have a clue, do you? Dad, she was cheating on you.' The words came out sharp, like breaking glass. I couldn't believe he still didn't get it.
grounded_facts: Alexandra says
You really don't have a clue, do you?
Alexandra says
Dad, she was cheating on you.
The statement follows Matt's suggestion that she help inform others about Elizabeth's condition.

memories.memory_text: I told him I saw her go into the house with that guy, his hand on her ass, and how it made me sick. I remember how quiet he got—like the air got sucked out of the room.
grounded_facts: Alexandra describes seeing her mother enter a house with a man
She mentions the man had his hand on her mother's ass
She states it made her sick and that she confronted her mother afterward.

memories.memory_text: I looked at Dad and said, 'Dad, this is Sid,' like it was obvious he needed to know who Sid was, even if he didn’t want to.
grounded_facts: Alexandra introduces Sid to her father Matt at the swimming pool. The introduction happens after Matt arrives and observes Alexandra with Scottie and Sid. Alexandra initiates the social moment despite the tense atmosphere. The line 'Dad
this is Sid' is spoken directly by Alexandra in the script.

memories.memory_text: When Dad tried to brush Sid off, I told him, 'Sid's coming with us, okay?'—not asking, just stating it, because I wasn’t going to let him decide who I could be around.
grounded_facts: After Matt indicates the visit is for family only
Alexandra insists Sid come along. Her statement 'Sid's coming with us
okay?' is a direct challenge to Matt’s authority. The phrasing is assertive
using 'okay' not as a question but as a boundary. The script shows Matt objecting on Sid’s behalf
but Alexandra overrides it.

memories.memory_text: I told Dad that Sid knows everything, standing right there with Matt approaching the car, and I meant it—he’s part of this now, no matter how much Dad wants to control who’s in the loop.
grounded_facts: Alexandra says 'He knows everything' in response to Matt asking if Sid knows what they're doing.
The scene takes place outside the King house, as Matt approaches the car where Alexandra and Sid are.
Sid makes a comment about Matt's wife dying and being cheated on, showing he is aware of the family situation.

memories.memory_text: I asked Gramps if he was okay when he tossed the papers and stared off like he was somewhere else—I could see he was unraveling, and someone had to say something.
grounded_facts: Alexandra asked Scott, 'Gramps, are you okay?' after he threw down the documents and became distant.
Scott was visibly distressed while reading Elizabeth's advanced directive, making bitter remarks about family members.
Alexandra spoke up during a tense moment involving legal documents and emotional outbursts.

memories.memory_text: I didn't tell Mom I got my period for almost a year because she always wanted me to act older, and when it finally happened, I thought I'd shat myself.
grounded_facts: Alexandra did not inform her mother about getting her period for approximately a year.
She felt pressure from her mother to look and act older.
Her first period was confusing and embarrassing, leading her to believe she had soiled herself.

memories.memory_text: I asked if I should go ring the bell, just to see what would happen, but Matt shut it down fast.
grounded_facts: Alexandra suggested ringing the doorbell at Brian Speer's house.
Matt initially seemed tempted but then refused, calling the situation stupid.

memories.memory_text: Before that, I told them we should Google Brian Speer later—something concrete to do instead of just sitting there staring at that sad little house.
grounded_facts: Alexandra proposed researching Brian Speer online after arriving at the house.
She identified the house as the location where she previously saw her mother with Brian Speer, though not with certainty that he lived there.

memories.memory_text: I saw Brian Speer’s face on that real estate sign and told Dad to go back — I knew it was him the second we passed.
grounded_facts: Alexandra noticed the real estate sign with Brian Speer's photo while in the car.
She instructed her father to reverse the car to go back to the sign.
She identified Brian Speer from the photo on the sign before anyone else did.

memories.memory_text: I got out of the car and took a photo of his face on the flyer — needed proof, and no one else was going to do it.
grounded_facts: Alexandra exited the vehicle after identifying the photo.
She physically approached the 'For Sale' sign.
She used her phone to photograph Brian Speer's image on the flyer.

memories.memory_text: I told Mom I was sorry for being bad, for wasting her money on private schools, for everything — and then Matt spanked me. I couldn’t believe it, standing there in the hospital room, and all I could say was, 'Did you just spank me?'
grounded_facts: Alexandra apologized to her mother for past behavior and financial burden.
Matt spanked Alexandra in response to her tone toward Elizabeth.
Alexandra questioned Matt's action with disbelief, asking if he had just spanked her.

memories.memory_text: After the spanking, I leaned in and told Mom I always wanted to be like her — that I *am* like her, exactly like her — even if it sounded dramatic. I meant it, even if I couldn’t say it without sarcasm.
grounded_facts: Alexandra admitted to wanting to be like her mother and acknowledged a personal identification with her.
She expressed this realization aloud at her mother’s bedside, despite the emotional weight.
She self-consciously noted the dramatic tone of her own statement.

memories.memory_text: I stood in the doorway and told Dad to drop it, that he had bigger things to deal with than chasing down Brian Speer. I asked if he was recording, not because I cared about the deposition, but because I couldn’t believe he was focusing on work at a time like this.
grounded_facts: Alexandra questioned Matt about whether he was recording, after seeing him with a dictaphone.
She told Matt to 'drop it' regarding confronting Brian Speer, arguing he had more important issues.

memories.memory_text: After seeing the look on Dad’s face—like he actually had a plan for once—I said I wanted to go too. When he tried to shut me down, I shot back, 'Fuck that. Let's all get out of town,' because sitting around pretending everything was normal was worse than whatever mess we’d drive into.
grounded_facts: Alexandra observed Matt's resolve and responded with a rare smile before declaring, 'Then I want to go too.'
She insisted on joining the trip despite Matt’s refusal, countering with 'Fuck that. Let's all get out of town.'

memories.memory_text: Cousin Ralph nodded at me without knowing my name, just another face in the family he can't be bothered to remember.
grounded_facts: Cousin Ralph greeted Alexandra along with Scottie without recalling her name.
Cousin Ralph interacted with Matt and acknowledged the daughters briefly during a conversation about family matters and land trust decisions.

memories.memory_text: I told Dad I stopped using because boarding school made it impossible to get anything — no big deal, just how it went.
grounded_facts: Alexandra states she is no longer using drugs. She attributes her cessation to the restrictions of boarding school limiting access. She downplays the significance of stopping drug use. She clarifies that the lack of access
not personal choice
was the primary factor. The conversation occurs on a hotel lanai after news about her mother’s condition. She is smoking a cigarette at the start of the scene. Her father brings up drug use directly
prompting her response. She distinguishes her past drug use from ongoing behavior. She references Sid to deflect comparison. She acknowledges occasional pot use hypothetically
not currently. She admits to some drinking when pressed. She frames her honesty as a result of her mother’s dying condition.

memories.memory_text: I cut in because the conversation was getting too soft, too fake—mimicking Scottie, telling Matt to stop babying her, just shut it all down.
grounded_facts: Alexandra interrupts a conversation between Matt and Scottie about their mother.
She mocks the emotional tone by mimicking Scottie's question in a sarcastic voice.
She tells Matt to stop babying Scottie and says 'Can we drop this? Jesus.'
Her intervention stops the emotional exchange.

memories.memory_text: I told Scottie Reina’s a fucked-up ho-bag and he needs to stay away from her—she’s going to end up a meth-head, used by stupid guys. I made him say it: she’s a twat.
grounded_facts: Alexandra explicitly labels Reina as a
fucked-up ho-bag" and warns Scottie about her influence. She predicts Reina will become a meth addict and be exploited by men. She instructs Scottie to repeat that Reina is a "twat
which he does. The warning occurs in the context of Scottie showing interest in adult movies, prompted by Reina’s behavior. ],
tags": [ "warning
protective
confrontational
moral judgment
influence

memories.memory_text: When Scottie said he wanted to end up like me, I had to clarify—I meant the earlier me, the one who was mean and yelled at Mom. I didn’t want him to confuse who I am now with who I used to be.
grounded_facts: Scottie responds positively to Alexandra by saying he wants to end up like her. Alexandra immediately corrects him
specifying she was referring to her past self—the one who was hostile and aggressive. She distinguishes her current self from her earlier
more destructive behavior. The correction is prompted by her own warning about Reina mirroring that past version of herself.

memories.memory_text: I watched my dad talk to that woman from a distance, standing there pretending to just be looking at the water, but I knew he was trying to find something out. When he said Hugh King was his cousin and she mentioned Brian Speer, I could see it hit him—like he was holding back something heavy.
grounded_facts: Alexandra observes Matt speaking with Julie Speer near the surf.
Matt mentions Hugh King is his cousin.
Julie responds by mentioning her husband, Brian Speer.
Matt suppresses a reaction, which Alexandra witnesses.

memories.scene_title: 99、EXT. THE ROAD BY THE BAY - SUNSET
memories.memory_text: I remember pushing Dad to act right then—'So let's do it right now. Let's go knock on his door'—and feeling that sharp rush of satisfaction when he agreed, like we were finally moving instead of just talking.
grounded_facts: Alexandra proposed confronting Don Holitzer immediately upon learning of his connection to Brian Speer.
She expressed quiet exultation when Matt confirmed the plan, which he then cautioned against.

memories.scene_title: 99、EXT. THE ROAD BY THE BAY - SUNSET
memories.memory_text: I called Sid over and told them the plan—Dad and I would go talk to the Holitzers while they took Scottie back to the hotel, and I remember how fast Sid shut down Scottie's protest with a joke about TV.
grounded_facts: Alexandra organized the team split by instructing Sid to take Scottie back to the hotel.
She announced the plan during a group huddle, framing the trip as Dad going to talk to 'some friends'.
Scottie initially objected but was diverted by Sid's suggestion to watch TV.

memories.memory_text: I told Matt, 'Don't be a pussy,' right before we walked up to Julie Speer. He was freezing, stammering about what to say, and I couldn't let him blow this.
grounded_facts: Alexandra said 'Don't be a pussy' to Matt as they approached the cottage.
Matt expressed hesitation and uncertainty about what to say before encountering Julie Speer.

memories.memory_text: I saw Julie come out with hamburger patties and elbowed Matt hard—this was the moment, no backing out. I didn't speak then, but I made sure he moved forward.
grounded_facts: Julie Speer exited the cottage with a plate of hamburger patties.
Alexandra nudged Matt just before Julie noticed them.

memories.memory_text: I cut Sid off when he started talking about exposing the affair just to make himself feel righteous—he wasn’t thinking about Scottie at all.
grounded_facts: Alexandra says 'Sid.' to interrupt him during the airplane conversation.
Sid makes a comment about telling the wife as a moral obligation, using crude language.
Alexandra redirects the conversation to Scottie, saying she’s the one they need to worry about now.

memories.memory_text: I stood up and said my dad’s been doing an amazing job, because no one else was going to say it while Grandpa Scott tore into him right here in Mom’s room.
grounded_facts: Alexandra speaks in defense of her father, Matt, during a tense exchange with Scott Thorson.
The statement occurs in the hospice room while Elizabeth is present and unresponsive.
Scott Thorson is questioning Matt about the land sale and implying blame for Elizabeth's condition.
No one else intervenes verbally before Alexandra speaks, and her line directly follows Sid's failed attempt to defuse the situation.

memories.memory_text: I felt Matt and Sid step toward me, arms open, but I didn’t want it—I let them hug me for a second anyway, just to get through it. My body was heavy, like I was walking through water, and all I could think was that Scottie still hadn’t seen her.
grounded_facts: Alexandra emerges from Elizabeth's room with a distant, sad expression.
Matt and Sid attempt to comfort her by rising and approaching for hugs.
She allows brief physical contact but does not reciprocate or engage emotionally.

memories.memory_text: I crouched slightly to meet Scottie’s eyes, then held out my hand—she looked so small, so scared. I guided her toward the room, saying something soft, something steady, even though my chest felt like it was cracking open.
grounded_facts: Alexandra signals to Matt that she will handle Scottie.
She approaches Scottie and physically escorts her toward Elizabeth's room.
She provides verbal reassurance while leading her sister inside.

memories.memory_text: I sat down next to Matt, pulled the quilt over my legs, and asked for some ice cream. He didn’t say anything, just handed me his bowl without looking away from the TV.
grounded_facts: Alexandra sits next to Matt on the sofa.
She pulls the quilt over her legs.
She asks, 'Can I have some?'
Matt hands her his ice cream without looking away from the TV.

memories.memory_text: I sat there with my papers spread around me, looking up at Elizabeth—her body stiff in that bed, tubes in her nose and throat, machines breathing for her—and I said her name out loud, like I was trying to make it real: this is Elizabeth King, my wife.
grounded_facts: Matt is in a hospital room with documents around him, having brought work to the bedside.
Elizabeth is on a hospital bed with a feeding tube, ventilator, and IVs.
Matt internally identifies Elizabeth as his wife while observing her comatose state.
Elizabeth has been in a coma for 23 days after a boating accident.
Matt uses clinical terms (Glasgow scale, Rancho Los Amigos scale) to describe her condition, indicating his attempt to process it through facts.

memories.memory_text: I sat there listening to the neurologist describe Elizabeth’s reflexes as primitive and inconsistent, and all I could think was how she used to say I was the one who reacted without purpose, going through the motions.
grounded_facts: Matt is present in the neurologist's office receiving a diagnosis about his wife.
The neurologist describes Elizabeth's responses as non-purposeful, inconsistent, and primitive.
Matt has an internal thought connecting the clinical description to a past accusation from his wife.

memories.memory_text: I yanked the phone from Scottie’s hands after she refused to tell me what she’d said to her mother — all that giggling while her mom lies there unconscious, it made me snap.
grounded_facts: Matt confronts Scottie about texting instead of talking to her mother.
Scottie says she spoke to her mother but claims it's private.
Matt demands the phone and takes it from her after a physical struggle.
The interaction ends with Matt telling Scottie to say goodbye before leaving.

memories.memory_text: I just want to be a normal guy — not another link in this chain of ancestors staring down from the walls, their lives dictating mine.
grounded_facts: Matt is in his study at night, surrounded by photos and memorabilia of his ancestors.
He is looking at portraits of Edward King and Princess Margaret Ke'alohilani while reflecting on their legacy.
He expresses a desire to no longer be controlled by his family's historical and financial legacy.
The clock reads 1:20, indicating the late hour and suggesting isolation or private reflection.

memories.memory_text: I sat there across from Dr. Johnston, hearing him say the words — no eye movement, no brainstem reflexes, she'll never be like she was — and I just stayed still, not moving, like if I didn’t react, it wouldn’t be true.
grounded_facts: Matt is in a consultation room with Dr. Sam Johnston, a family friend and physician.
Dr. Johnston informs Matt that his wife Elizabeth has no brainstem reflexes and her condition is irreversible.
Matt asks how long she will last after life support is withdrawn, and Dr. Johnston responds it could be days to two weeks.
Dr. Johnston mentions organ donation and advising friends to say goodbye, noting Elizabeth had many people in her life.
Matt does not move or visibly react during the conversation, remaining physically still.

memories.memory_text: Troy approached our table, asking about Elizabeth with this intense, personal concern—saying he'd visited her, that her hand moved when he spoke, like it meant something. I stared him down because something about the way he said it, like he was close to her, set me off.
grounded_facts: Troy Cook approached Matt and Scottie at the restaurant.
Troy mentioned visiting Elizabeth in the hospital.
Troy claimed Elizabeth's hand moved when he spoke to her.
Matt stared intensely at Troy, causing him to avert his gaze.
Matt felt discomfort and suspicion about Troy's familiarity with Elizabeth's condition.

memories.memory_text: After Troy left, Scottie snapped at him—comparing Mom's twitches to a dead chicken running around—and then just got up and walked away. I watched her go, and I didn’t even look back at Troy; I just followed her, feeling how alone we both were in it.
grounded_facts: Scottie responded to Troy by dismissing the significance of Elizabeth's hand movement.
Scottie used a graphic analogy involving a decapitated chicken.
Scottie abruptly left the table and headed toward the pool.
Matt followed Scottie without acknowledging Troy again.
Matt perceived Scottie's emotional distress and withdrawal.

memories.memory_text: I found Alexandra laughing in the moonlight, drunk with a golf club in her hand, acting like it was all a big joke—her friend had just fallen in the grass with a bottle, and she kept saying 'My fucking dad is here!' like it was the funniest thing.
grounded_facts: Matt arrives at the soccer field at night with Scottie and the dorm supervisor.
Alexandra is present with Emily, both are out of bounds and intoxicated.
Alexandra laughs upon seeing Matt and mocks his presence.
Emily falls while trying to run, holding a bottle and a golf club.
Matt expresses frustration about paying tuition for such behavior.

memories.memory_text: When I told her she needed to come home to see her mother, she didn't say anything—just threw her golf club into the dark and screamed 'Fuck Mom!'—and I just stood there, hearing my own voice in my head asking why the women in my life keep falling apart.
grounded_facts: Matt tells Alexandra she must come home to see her comatose mother.
Alexandra reacts by throwing her golf club and cursing.
The dorm supervisor reprimands her for her language.
Matt internally reflects on Elizabeth’s self-destructive behavior.

memories.memory_text: Alexandra told me, 'Dad, she was cheating on you,' and said she saw Mom go into a house with some guy who had his hand on her ass. I couldn't process it—kept asking if she was sure, what it meant, who the man was, but she didn't know.
grounded_facts: Alexandra reveals that Matt's wife was having an affair.
She claims she saw Matt's wife entering a house with a man whose hand was on her ass.
Matt responds with disbelief and repeated questioning, showing shock and confusion.

memories.memory_text: After she told me everything, I stood up, walked in a circle, didn't know where to go or what to do—then I just said, 'Watch Scottie,' like that was the only thing I could hold onto.
grounded_facts: Matt stands up and walks in a small circle, disoriented after hearing the revelation.
He gives the directive 'Watch Scottie' as a way to regain control or focus.
The action follows a moment of emotional overwhelm and silence.

memories.memory_text: I was walking away when Mark called out the name—Brian Speer. Two e's. That detail stuck, like it made it more real, more permanent.
grounded_facts: Matt was leaving the Mitchell house when Mark provided the name of Elizabeth's lover.
Mark specifically mentioned the spelling of the last name: 'Two e's.'
The name 'Brian Speer' was new information for Matt in this moment.

memories.memory_text: I pulled the car over across from that ordinary house and just stared at it—plain walls, no fence, nothing remarkable—and asked, 'There? He lives there?' It didn't look like a place that could break my life apart.
grounded_facts: Matt pulls the car over across from a house identified as possibly belonging to Brian Speer.
Matt questions whether Brian Speer actually lives in the house, expressing disbelief.
The house is described in the scene as 'supremely average-looking.'

memories.memory_text: I sat there with the engine off, feeling the weight of what we were doing—crouching when a plumbing truck passed, debating whether to ring the doorbell—and then finally started the car, saying, 'Okay, that's enough. We're getting out of here,' because staying felt pointless and degrading.
grounded_facts: Matt decides against confronting or approaching the house after hesitation.
He rejects the idea of stalking or lingering, calling it 'stupid.'
He restarts the car and ends the surveillance, asserting control over the situation.

memories.memory_text: I crouched by her bed and asked her if she was really going to leave me for Brian Speer—called her a liar, told her she was still ruining everything, even lying there on a ventilator.
grounded_facts: Matt confronts his comatose wife about her affair and planned divorce.
He expresses anger and betrayal, questioning her identity and the truth of their marriage.
He states that he had also considered divorcing her someday.

memories.memory_text: I picked up her childhood doll and threw it, muttering 'Daddy's little girl'—suddenly disgusted by the things they'd brought to make her seem innocent, while I was left holding the truth no one else knew.
grounded_facts: Matt notices personal items like a doll, diplomas, medals, and a quilt placed around his wife’s bed.
He reacts to the doll by tossing it and uttering 'Daddy's little girl' with bitterness.
His gesture reflects skepticism toward the sentimental narrative being constructed around her.

memories.memory_text: I stood in the living room and told everyone that Elizabeth wasn't going to make it, that we were taking her off life support the next day — her wishes — and I could feel my voice breaking as I said I wanted to tell them in person.
grounded_facts: Matt announces to family and friends that Elizabeth's coma is permanent and she will be taken off life support the next day.
Matt states he wanted to deliver the news in person to their dear friends.
Matt acknowledges Elizabeth's wishes as the reason for removing life support.
Matt becomes emotionally overwhelmed, nearly losing composure when speaking.

memories.memory_text: I asked Alex if she’d been using drugs, and she shot me a look before stomping out her cigarette—then admitted she’d stopped, mostly because boarding school cut off her access.
grounded_facts: Matt asks Alexandra if she has been using drugs.
Alexandra initially denies use, then clarifies she stopped due to lack of access at boarding school.
Matt notices the smell of pot on Sid and questions Alexandra about her drug use.

memories.memory_text: After she admitted the truth, I told her I was proud—she’s here helping with Scottie and me, and that meant something, even if she brushed it off.
grounded_facts: Matt expresses pride in Alexandra for being honest.
Matt acknowledges Alexandra’s support with Scottie and the family situation.
Alexandra downplays the significance of her honesty and presence.

memories.memory_text: I saw sand stuffed in Scottie’s bikini top and told her to take it out—Alex and Sid didn’t stop her, and I had to be the one to say something, again.
grounded_facts: Matt notices lumps of sand in Scottie's bikini top.
Matt tells Scottie to remove the sand immediately.
Matt expresses frustration toward Alex and Sid for not intervening.

memories.memory_text: I was jogging at dawn along Hanalei Bay, out of breath and pushing through the burn, when I saw a man in a Stanford Lacrosse shirt coming toward me — and something in his stride or the set of his shoulders made me slow, then stop, then turn around to follow.
grounded_facts: Matt was jogging on the beach at sunrise.
He passed a man wearing a 'Stanford Lacrosse' T-shirt.
The man was Brian Speer.
Matt did not recognize him immediately but turned to follow after a moment of hesitation.

memories.memory_text: I kept muttering under my breath, 'Hi, you don't know me, but I know you,' over and over, like I was rehearsing it, and when I finally said it out loud — 'Hi, I'm Liz's husband' — he just looked at me and said 'That's right. Fuckface' before walking away.
grounded_facts: Matt repeated a phrase under his breath while following Brian.
He confronted Brian by identifying himself as Liz's husband.
Brian responded with 'That's right. Fuckface' and walked off.
The confrontation occurred near a cottage path lined by a hedge.

memories.memory_text: I was ready to confront him, but then I saw the woman in the white bathing suit and the two little boys come out — he has a family, just like me. I couldn't do it then.
grounded_facts: Matt observes Brian entering and exiting a cottage on a lawn.
Matt sees a woman in a white bathing suit and two little boys emerge after Brian.
Matt decides not to confront Brian upon realizing he has a family.

memories.memory_text: I told Brian Speer to his face that Elizabeth is dying — that we’d taken her off the machines that morning and she’d be gone in a few days. I said it flat, no warning, just the truth.
grounded_facts: Matt directly informs Brian that Elizabeth is dying and has been unhooked from life support.
The statement is delivered abruptly, with no softening, after initial pleasantries.

memories.memory_text: I watched his face freeze when I said her name — Brian’s grin dropped the second I mentioned Elizabeth. Alexandra and I looked at each other, then back at him, letting it sink in.
grounded_facts: Brian’s reaction is immediate and visible — his smile fades upon hearing Elizabeth’s name.
Matt and Alexandra share a deliberate glance, observing Brian’s stunned response.

memories.memory_text: I pushed Brian to tell me how it started—Super Bowl party at the Mitchells’—and I kept pressing because I needed to know what made someone cross that line, even if he said it just happened.
grounded_facts: Matt asks Brian how he and Elizabeth met.
Brian says they met at a Super Bowl party.
Matt connects it to the Mitchells’ house.
Matt insists 'Nothing just happens' and questions Brian’s motives.

memories.memory_text: I asked if he’d ever been in my bedroom, and when he said once, I felt a sharp sting—there was no reason for him to be in there, and I couldn’t believe she let him.
grounded_facts: Matt asks Brian if he has ever been inside his bedroom.
Brian admits he was there once.
Matt responds with disappointment, wishing Brian had lied.
The question occurs near the end of the conversation, after emotional buildup.

memories.memory_text: Scott Thorson walked in and started grilling me about the land deal right there in the hospice room, as if Elizabeth wasn't lying in the bed, as if this was the time or place. When he implied I was profiting from her accident, I could feel my hands tighten — I told him she deserved more, because I couldn't say anything else without breaking down.
grounded_facts: Scott Thorson questioned Matt about the land sale decision in the hospice room.
Scott made a comment suggesting Matt was gaining financially from Elizabeth's condition.
Matt responded defensively, saying 'Scott, what's your point?' and later acknowledged Elizabeth deserved more.
Matt dismissed his daughters and Sid from the room after the confrontation escalated.
The conversation occurred while Elizabeth was present and unresponsive in the hospital bed.

memories.memory_text: I sat at my desk in the silence, just the clock ticking past 3:20, staring at the blueprints and renderings spread out like a verdict I couldn’t escape.
grounded_facts: Matt was sitting at his desk late at night, indicated by the clock showing 3:20.
The only audible sound was the ticking of an old clock.
Blueprints and renderings were placed on the desk, which Matt glanced at while pacing.

memories.scene_title: 113、EXT. KING FAMILY SHARED PROPERTY HOUSE - LATER
memories.memory_text: I remember sitting at the table with Hugh and Six, watching the calabash go around, each cousin dropping in their ballot like it was some sacred ritual, and realizing none of us had earned that land—it was entrusted, not earned.
grounded_facts: Matt observes the ballot process with a calabash being passed among cousins.
Matt reflects on the unearned nature of the family's land ownership, stating it was 'entrusted to us.'
The moment occurs while seated with Cousin Hugh, Cousin Six, and an elderly cousin amid piles of contracts.

memories.scene_title: 113、EXT. KING FAMILY SHARED PROPERTY HOUSE - LATER
memories.memory_text: I remember Hugh handing me the pen, smiling like it was all settled, and I said no—I said I wouldn't sign, and I saw the look on his face, like I'd broken some unspoken rule.
grounded_facts: Cousin Hugh hands Matt a pen and urges him to sign the agreement.
Matt explicitly refuses, saying 'I can't do it. I won't do it. I'm not going to sign.'
Hugh reacts with confusion and concern, indicating surprise at Matt's defiance.

memories.memory_text: Julie Speer stood by Elizabeth's bed and said she forgave her—for trying to take Brian, for almost destroying her family—and I didn't know what to do, so I just stood there, then told her it was enough, that she should stop.
grounded_facts: Julie Speer approached Elizabeth's hospital bed and declared she forgave her for the affair with Brian.
Matt interrupted and told Julie it was enough, then opened the door for her to leave.
Matt did not stop Julie from speaking until after she finished her forgiveness statement.

memories.memory_text: After Julie left, I realized it was the last time I'd be alone with Elizabeth, so I took her hand, touched her hair, kissed her lips, and whispered goodbye—over and over—like I was finally letting her go.
grounded_facts: Matt remained alone with Elizabeth after Julie departed.
Matt touched Elizabeth's hand and hair, kissed her lips, and placed his hand on her stomach.
Matt said 'Goodbye, my love, my friend, my pain, my joy. Goodbye. Goodbye. Goodbye.'

memories.memory_text: I told Alex to go first, and I watched her hesitate, holding the scoop of ashes like she was afraid to let go—then she poured them in, and we all just stared as the water darkened and swallowed them.
grounded_facts: Matt instructed Alexandra to begin the ash scattering.
Alexandra opened the box, used the scoop, and poured the first portion of ashes into the ocean.
Matt observed her actions and the immediate effect of the ashes in the water.

memories.memory_text: After Scottie said goodbye, I took the box, looked down at what was left of Elizabeth, and poured the rest into the sea—then I dropped my lei in, feeling the weight lift just enough to breathe.
grounded_facts: Matt received the urn from Scottie after her turn.
He looked inside the urn before emptying the remaining ashes into the ocean.
He removed his lei and placed it in the water, followed by his daughters.

memories.memory_text: I handed Scottie her ice cream and got under the quilt with her.
grounded_facts: Matt gives Scottie a dish of ice cream with strawberry and mocha chip scoops.
Matt gets under the quilt with Scottie on the sofa.

memories.memory_text: Alex came in, saw the show, sat down beside me, and asked for some. Without looking away from the screen, I passed her my own ice cream. We just sat there, the three of us, as the credits rolled.
grounded_facts: Alexandra enters the room and sits next to Matt on the sofa.
Alexandra asks for ice cream.
Matt hands his ice cream to Alexandra without looking away from the TV.
The credits roll quietly over the family sitting together.

memories.memory_text: I was dancing outside the classroom, singing 'This shit is bananas' over and over with my earbuds in, and I didn’t even notice Dad or the teachers looking at me.
grounded_facts: Scottie was dancing just outside the classroom door with earbuds in.
She was repeatedly singing 'This shit is bananas. B-a-n-a-n-a-s!'
She remained oblivious to the adults observing her and did not respond when Ms. Hayashi spoke to her.

memories.memory_text: I told Dad I'm a real photographer, just like that famous lady who took pictures of her mom in the hospital, and that's what I'm doing for my art project.
grounded_facts: Scottie identifies herself as a 'photographer' and 'real photographer' in response to her father's criticism.
She references a book in which a famous female photographer documented her dying mother in a hospital.
She explicitly connects her art project—featuring her sick mother—to that precedent in professional photography.

memories.memory_text: I didn’t know what to say to Mom, so I just said I was hungry and wanted a Sprite — not diet, regular — and Matt had to drag me to the chair, but he kept pushing me to talk or write her something, like a letter, even though she’s in a coma and can’t read it.
grounded_facts: Scottie expresses not knowing what to say to her mother.
Scottie resists talking to her mother in the hospital room.
Matt suggests Scottie write a letter to her mother.
Scottie questions how her mother would read a letter while in a coma.
Scottie asks for a regular Sprite, not diet, and expresses concern about being seen as fat.
Matt insists Scottie stay and talk to her mother before leaving.

memories.memory_text: I remember Matt saying, 'Elizabeth's going to make it out okay,' like he really believed it — like Mom was gonna wake up and everything would just fix itself.
grounded_facts: Matt said 'Elizabeth's going to make it out okay' while walking up the hospital stairs.
The statement was made in a reflective, hopeful tone, suggesting belief in Elizabeth's recovery and family reunion.
Scottie was not present in the scene, so the memory must be secondhand or overheard later.

memories.memory_text: I told Dad it was private, and he got so mad—he even grabbed my phone, but I wasn’t gonna say what I said to Mom. It was just for her.
grounded_facts: Scottie had a private conversation with her mother in the hospital room.
Matt demanded to know what Scottie said to her mother.
Scottie refused to disclose the content, stating it was private.
Matt attempted to take control by snatching her phone, leading to a physical struggle.
Scottie asserted boundaries by withholding information despite pressure.

memories.memory_text: I saw those Melnick dorks walking with their stupid kayaks and matching shirts, and I just had to say it—'Dorks!'—and then laughed because it was so obvious they were dorks, and even Matt couldn't stop me from saying it twice.
grounded_facts: Scottie sees a family walking with kayaks and matching 'Melnick Family Reunion' shirts.
Scottie exclaims 'Dorks!' twice, first spontaneously and then again while laughing.
Matt tells Scottie to stop, but she continues laughing in the car seat.

memories.memory_text: I remember sitting outside Lani's house all day after she locked me out of her birthday party, while everyone else was inside having fun.
grounded_facts: Scottie was locked out of Lani's birthday party last year.
Scottie had to sit outside the house all day while others were inside.
Scottie associates this event with Lani thinking she was 'so great'.

memories.memory_text: I said 'Sorry' real quick, then nodded super big when Dad pushed me to show I meant it, even though I didn't feel sorry at all.
grounded_facts: Scottie says 'Sorry.'
Scottie makes a big exaggerated nod when prompted by Matt.

memories.memory_text: I asked Dad why it's called a jellyfish when it's not a fish and not jelly. I said I'm going to ask Mom when she wakes up.
grounded_facts: Scottie asks Matt why a jellyfish is called that despite not being a fish or jelly.
Scottie states she will ask Mom when she wakes up if she knows the answer.

memories.memory_text: I told Troy that just because Mom's hand moved doesn't mean she's getting better — it's like a chicken running after its head's cut off, still dead.
grounded_facts: Troy mentions Elizabeth’s hand moving during his visit, calling it an encouraging sign.
Scottie responds by rejecting the significance of the hand movement with a chicken analogy.
Scottie uses a blunt, graphic comparison to defend against false hope about her mother's condition.

memories.memory_text: I said I hated him, but then I told Dad I didn't mean to say the thing about the dead chicken. I just got scared 'cause I don't want Mom to die.
grounded_facts: Scottie says 'I hate him' in reference to someone previously mentioned.
Scottie apologizes for saying 'dead chicken,' indicating regret over the earlier comment.
Scottie explicitly states, 'I don't want Mom to die,' revealing emotional vulnerability.

memories.memory_text: Dad said we should drive to the airport and surprise Alex on the Big Island, and I said yeah—she should come home, and I'm out of school anyway.
grounded_facts: Matt suggests a spontaneous trip to the Big Island to surprise Alex.
Matt argues Alex should be with the family and asks if Scottie misses her.
Scottie agrees enthusiastically, saying 'She should be too!' and notes he is out of school.

memories.memory_text: I flipped off Troy as Matt and I walked away — I didn’t even think, I just did it, my hand popping up like it had its own mind.
grounded_facts: Scottie flips off Troy while leaving the outrigger restaurant with Matt.
Troy is described as crestfallen during the gesture.
The action occurs as Scottie and Matt are heading toward the exit.

memories.memory_text: I was so tired, just leaning against the wall when Matt knocked on the door to get Alex. I didn’t say much, but I remember the TV was still on in the background and I could barely keep my eyes open.
grounded_facts: Scottie is present in the hallway outside the dorm supervisor's room at night.
Scottie is exhausted and slouching against the wall.
The TV is on in the background during the interaction.
Matt speaks with the dorm supervisor about picking up Alexandra.

memories.memory_text: I ran up and yelled, 'Hi, Alex! It's me, Scottie!' and then I told her we came to get her to come home — I haven't been to school in three whole days.
grounded_facts: Scottie initiates contact with Alexandra by calling out to her on the soccer field.
Scottie informs Alexandra that they came to bring her home.
Scottie mentions not having gone to school for three days.

memories.memory_text: I said I don't like eggs when Matt brought them to me, and then I told him I thought they were for him, not me.
grounded_facts: Scottie rejected the scrambled eggs Matt offered.
Scottie clarified that she assumed the eggs were meant for Matt, not herself.

memories.memory_text: When Matt said they shouldn't talk in front of me, I said 'I don't mind' — I wanted them to know I can handle it.
grounded_facts: Matt expressed hesitation about speaking in front of Scottie.
Scottie responded with 'I don't mind' to indicate she was okay hearing the conversation.

memories.memory_text: I ran out in just my bra and panties, and when Matt told me to go change, I flipped him off and ran back inside.
grounded_facts: Scottie ran outside wearing only her bra and panties.
Alexandra reacted angrily, telling Scottie to get out of her underwear.
Matt told Scottie to go back inside and change into her swimsuit.
Scottie said 'Ooo, la la! Don't I look divine?'
Scottie flipped off Matt before running back inside.

memories.memory_text: I was laughing with Alex and Sid in the pool chairs, feeling good in my dumb shirt, until Dad showed up and everything got all stiff and serious.
grounded_facts: Scottie was present at the swimming pool with Alexandra and Sid.
Scottie was wearing an oversized t-shirt with provocative text.
Scottie stopped laughing when Matt arrived.
Scottie greeted Matt with 'Hi, Daddy.'

memories.memory_text: They were talking about taking me to a movie, like I didn’t hear them saying it was to get me out of the house.
grounded_facts: Alexandra states the purpose of going to a movie is to get Scottie out of the house.
Scottie is the reason given for the outing, indicating she is present or being discussed in context of being removed from the current environment.

memories.memory_text: Cousin Ralph said my name, sort of, when he saw us at the airport, but I think he forgot it right after — he called me something else, maybe Alex? I waved anyway because he was smiling and looked like he knew Dad.
grounded_facts: Cousin Ralph greets Scottie and Alexandra by name, though he clearly forgets their names.
Scottie is present during the interaction between Matt and Cousin Ralph at the airport.
Cousin Ralph is described as grinning and nodding while greeting the children.

memories.memory_text: I took my shoes off and slid across the super shiny floor—it was so slick, like ice, but warm. I giggled when I almost crashed into the big plant.
grounded_facts: Scottie removed her shoes in the hotel lobby
Scottie slid across the shiny marble floor
The floor was reflective and smooth, allowing for sliding

memories.memory_text: I put sand in my bikini top and called them my beach boobs, but then Dad saw and got mad, and Mom and Sid told me to take them out, so I stomped to the water pulling it all out.
grounded_facts: Scottie stuffed sand into her bikini top
She referred to the sand-filled bikini as 'my beach boobs'
Matt ordered her to remove the sand immediately
Alex and Sid told her to take it out, with Alex calling her 'stupid'
Scottie scowled and physically removed the sand while walking toward the water

memories.memory_text: I asked Dad if we could swim with sharks in a cage like in the hotel magazine, but Mom shut me down fast, saying we weren't here for stuff like that.
grounded_facts: Scottie approached Matt to ask about swimming with sharks
She referenced reading about a shark cage experience in a hotel magazine
She expressed desire to do the shark activity
Alex dismissed the idea immediately, stating they weren't there for such activities

memories.memory_text: I asked Dad how he and Mom met, and if he thought she was pretty, and what he loved about her—my voice felt shaky even though I tried to sound casual, and then Alex snapped at me to shut up.
grounded_facts: Scottie asked her father how he and her mother met.
Scottie asked if her father thought her mother was pretty.
Scottie asked what her father loved about her mother.
Alexandra interrupted and told Scottie to stop, mocking her questions.

memories.memory_text: I grabbed the remote and saw those movie ads with women in sexy clothes — I said, 'Let's watch one of these!' just to see what would happen.
grounded_facts: Scottie sees adult movie promotions on TV while flipping channels with Alexandra
Scottie initiates watching an adult movie by saying
Let's watch one of these!

twat

She's a twat" and then runs across the room chanting "Twat

twat!

memories.memory_text: I was sleeping in the window seat on the plane, so I don’t remember anyone talking about me, but I can sort of feel them looking over.
grounded_facts: Scottie is asleep in the window seat during the scene.
The adults turn to look at Scottie while discussing telling him about his mother's infidelity.
Scottie is not awake to hear the conversation, but the script notes the group's attention turns toward him.

memories.memory_text: Dr. Herman handed me a little rubber octopus that squeaked, and said it was smart like a dog and used ink to hide from danger.
grounded_facts: Dr. Herman gives Scottie a squeaky rubber octopus
Dr. Herman explains that octopuses are intelligent and use defense mechanisms like ink and camouflage

memories.memory_text: I asked if Mom was getting better because she wasn't on any machines, and no one answered — they just looked at each other.
grounded_facts: Scottie notices her mother is off life support and asks if she's getting better
The adults do not answer but exchange glances
